examples.q: A 5 kg ball moves at 8 m/s to the right. What is its linear momentum?
examples.solution:
Linear momentum is the product of an object's
mass and velocity.

Formula:
    p = m × v

where  p = momentum  (kg·m/s)
       m = mass       (kg)
       v = velocity   (m/s)

Momentum is a VECTOR — it has direction.

Step 1 — Identify the values:
    m = 5 kg
    v = 8 m/s (right)

Step 2 — Substitute and solve:
    p = 5 × 8
    p = 40 kg·m/s

Answer:  p = 40 kg·m/s, to the right.

examples.q: A 3 kg object is moving at 2 m/s. A force of 6 N is applied for 4 seconds in the same direction. What is the final velocity?
examples.solution:
Impulse is the change in momentum caused by a force
acting over time.

Formula:
    J = F × t = Δp = m × (vf − vi)

Step 1 — Calculate the impulse:
    J = F × t = 6 × 4 = 24 N·s

Step 2 — Apply the impulse-momentum theorem:
    J = m × (vf − vi)
    24 = 3 × (vf − 2)
    24 / 3 = vf − 2
    8 = vf − 2
    vf = 10 m/s

Answer:  vf = 10 m/s

examples.q: A 4 kg ball moving right at 6 m/s collides with a stationary 4 kg ball. After the collision they move together. What is their combined velocity?
examples.solution:
This is a perfectly inelastic collision.
Use Conservation of Momentum:

    Total momentum before = Total momentum after
    m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁ + m₂) × vf

Step 1 — Identify values:
    m₁ = 4 kg,  v₁ = 6 m/s
    m₂ = 4 kg,  v₂ = 0 m/s (stationary)

Step 2 — Substitute:
    (4 × 6) + (4 × 0) = (4 + 4) × vf
    24 + 0 = 8 × vf
    vf = 24 / 8
    vf = 3 m/s

Answer:  vf = 3 m/s, to the right.

questions.q: What is the momentum of a 10 kg object moving at 5 m/s?
A force of 10 N acts on an object for 3 seconds. What is the impulse?
questions.given:
Formula:  J = F × t
Unit of impulse: N·s (Newton-second)

questions.q: A 2 kg object at rest is hit with an impulse of 16 N·s. What is its final velocity?
questions.given:
Impulse-Momentum Theorem:  J = m × (vf − vi)
Object starts at rest: vi = 0.

questions.q: Which of the following has greater momentum: a 2 kg object at 10 m/s or a 5 kg object at 3 m/s?
questions.given:
Formula:  p = m × v
Calculate each momentum and compare.
questions.sol: p₁ = 2 × 10 = 20 kg·m/s. p₂ = 5 × 3 = 15 kg·m/s. The 2 kg object has greater momentum.

questions.q: A 6 kg object moves from 4 m/s to 10 m/s. What impulse caused this change?
questions.given:
Impulse = change in momentum
    J = m × (vf − vi)

questions.q: A 1 500 kg car moves at 20 m/s. What is its momentum?
According to the Law of Conservation of Momentum, what is true in a closed system with no external forces?
questions.given:
A 'closed system' means no forces act from outside.
This is one of the most fundamental laws in physics.
opts: Total momentum before = Total momentum after
Momentum only conserved in elastic collisions
questions.sol: The Law of Conservation of Momentum states: in a closed system, total momentum before any collision equals total momentum after.

questions.q: A 3 kg ball at 10 m/s collides with a 7 kg stationary ball and they stick together. What is the combined velocity?
questions.given:
Conservation of Momentum:
    m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁ + m₂) × vf

questions.q: Why does a longer contact time reduce the force of an impact (e.g., an airbag)?
questions.given:
Impulse:  J = F × t = Δp
If Δp (change in momentum) is fixed, what happens to F as t increases?
opts: F decreases because F = Δp / t — same impulse over more time
F increases because more time means more force
F is unchanged — only momentum changes
F increases because the object decelerates faster
questions.sol: J = F × t is fixed (same Δp). So F = J / t — if t increases, F must decrease. This is why airbags save lives.

questions.q: A 5 kg object moving at 4 m/s right collides head-on with a 5 kg object at 4 m/s left. They stick together. What is the final velocity?
questions.given:
Assign right = positive, left = negative.
Conservation of Momentum:  m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁ + m₂) × vf
